label: Fiery Cross Reef (Yongshu Jiao)
sub: China's primary Spratly command hub with 3,125m runway, hardened aircraft shelters for 24+ fighters, HQ-9B SAMs, and YJ-12B anti-ship missiles. Reclamation of ~677 acres completed 2015.

label: Fiery Cross — HQ-9B SAM Site
sub: HQ-9B long-range surface-to-air missile batteries deployed May 2018. Coverage radius ~160 nm; threatens all aircraft over the central South China Sea. Violates Xi Jinping's 2015 pledge to Obama not to militarize the features.

label: Mischief Reef (Meiji Jiao)
sub: Largest of China's three major Spratly outposts (~1,379 acres reclaimed). Constructed within the Philippines' EEZ; seizure from Philippines in 1995 was a turning point in SCS militarization. HQ-9B and YJ-12B weapons deployed since 2018.

label: Mischief Reef — Artillery Emplacements
sub: CSIS AMTI (2023) documented three sets of three hardened emplacements along the western and northern edges, suitable for mobile artillery or rocket launchers. Equipment periodically deployed under tarps. Part of ongoing Spratly Island fortification program.

label: Subi Reef (Zhubi Jiao)
sub: 976 acres reclaimed; located 16 nm southwest of Philippine-held Thitu Island. China's northernmost major Spratly outpost. HQ-9B SAMs and YJ-12B ASCMs deployed 2018. Two new radomes added 2023–2024 for ISR coverage of the SCS.

label: Subi Reef — Airstrip & Hangar Complex
sub: 3,000m runway with aircraft hangars. ISR and EW upgrades 2023–2024 include fixed monopole antenna arrays targeting multiple electromagnetic spectrum bands. AMTI: represents China's 'integrated strategy to establish unmatched ISR dominance.'

label: Johnson South Reef (Chigua Jiao)
sub: ~10 acres reclaimed. Site of March 14, 1988 'Johnson South Reef Skirmish' in which China killed approximately 70 Vietnamese sailors and seized the reef—China's first armed seizure of a Spratly feature. Now a small military outpost with anti-aircraft weapons.

label: Gaven Reef North (Nanxun Jiao)
sub: 86 acres reclaimed. Armed with anti-aircraft guns, naval guns, and search radars. Lighthouse built. China has occupied Gaven Reef since 1988; current outpost hosts barracks, radar systems, and communications equipment.

label: Hughes Reef (Dongmen Jiao)
sub: 7.6 acres reclaimed; Chinese outpost since 1988. Armed with anti-aircraft weapons. One of six Spratly features occupied by China before the major 2013–2016 reclamation drive. Receives Chinese maritime militia vessels operating in the Spratly waters.

label: Woody Island (Yongxing Dao) — China Paracel HQ
sub: China's largest Paracel outpost and seat of Sansha City administration (established 2012). 213 ha; 1,000+ military/civilian residents; 2,700m runway. HQ-9 SAMs deployed since February 2016, triggering US protests. China administers all Paracel Islands from this island.

label: Woody Island — Military Airfield
sub: 2,700m runway capable of hosting J-11 fighters, H-6 bombers, and KJ-500 AEW aircraft. China has deployed fighter jets to Woody Island for exercises, directly threatening Vietnam's SCS claims. Also hosts long-range radar systems monitoring Paracel and northern Spratly waters.

label: Yulin Naval Base — Hainan Island
sub: China's primary South Sea Fleet submarine base, with underground submarine pens carved into the mountain. Houses Type 094 Jin-class SSBNs and Type 093 Shang-class SSNs. Key forward deployment point for PLAN submarines operating in the South China Sea.

label: Zhanjiang — PLA South Sea Fleet HQ
sub: Headquarters of the PLA Navy's Southern Theater Command Navy (South Sea Fleet). Major surface combatant homeport. Zhanjiang Naval Academy also located here. Primary command and logistics center for China's South China Sea operations.

label: Pattle Island — China Paracel Outpost
sub: Second-largest island in China's Paracel Island chain. Hosts a weather station, military garrison, and communications equipment. China has administered the Paracel Islands since seizing them from South Vietnam in a 1974 battle that killed dozens of Vietnamese sailors.

label: Duncan Island — China Paracel Outpost
sub: Small island in the southwestern Paracel group; occupied by China since 1974. Hosts a military garrison and monitoring equipment. Vietnam continues to claim the entire Paracel Islands group as 'Hoang Sa archipelago.'

label: Triton Island — Southern Paracel Outpost
sub: Southernmost major island of the Paracel group. China-occupied since 1974. Site of controversial April 2020 Vietnamese fishing boat sinking by CCG vessel Haijing 4301, which rammed and sank QNg-90617 TS in waters near this island.

label: China Coast Guard — South Sea Base
sub: CCG southern operations center in the Guangdong/Hainan region. Coordinates CCG patrols, intercept operations, and law enforcement activities throughout the South China Sea. China's CCG has 500+ vessels fleet-wide, the world's largest coast guard.

label: Spratly Islands — EW/ISR Network Hub
sub: Coordinated electronic warfare and intelligence surveillance network across all three major Chinese Spratly outposts. New EW systems identified 2023–2024 at Fiery Cross, Subi, and Mischief Reefs target different electromagnetic bands. AMTI calls it an 'integrated strategy for unmatched ISR dominance.'

label: Hainan Island — PLA Military Complex
sub: China's key forward base for South China Sea operations. Hosts Yulin Naval Base (nuclear submarines), Sanya air base (H-6 bombers, surveillance aircraft), and multiple radar/signals intelligence installations. China announced Hainan's first jet carrier CNS Fujian completed sea trials from Hainan shipyards.

label: Sansha City Administrative Center
sub: China established Sansha City on Woody Island in July 2012 to administer all South China Sea island groups. In April 2020, China further subdivided it into Xisha District (Paracels) and Nansha District (Spratlys)—a formal administrative assertion of sovereignty over disputed waters. Vietnam and Philippines both protested.

label: BRP Sierra Madre — Second Thomas Shoal
sub: WWII-era US Navy tank landing ship deliberately grounded at Second Thomas Shoal (Ayungin Shoal) in 1999. Garrisoned by ~12 Philippine Marines. Hull in critical condition with large holes and severe corrosion. China has blocked/harassed at least 10 resupply missions since 2021 using water cannons, ramming, and in June 2024, bladed weapons.

label: Pag-asa Island (Thitu) — Philippines
sub: Philippines' largest occupied Spratly feature (37.2 ha). Only Spratly island with a civilian population; hosts Rancudo Airfield and Naval Station Emilio Liwanag. Surrounded by Chinese maritime militia since 2017. Philippines upgraded its airstrip in 2019 over Chinese protests. Located 16 nm from China's Subi Reef outpost.

label: Scarborough Shoal (Panatag / Huangyan)
sub: De facto Chinese control since 2012 standoff. 2016 PCA ruling found it a 'rock' not entitled to EEZ; China rejects ruling. CCG continuously present at lagoon entrance; Filipino fishermen routinely harassed with water cannons. Analysts warn Chinese permanent construction here would complete a strategic triangle with Paracels and Spratlys.

label: Whitsun Reef (Julian Felipe Reef) — 2021 Swarming
sub: ~220 Chinese blue-hulled vessels occupied this boomerang-shaped reef (12 km long) beginning December 2020, discovered by Philippines March 7, 2021. Vessels made no fishing activity, maintained military-style formations. Philippines filed 84 protests; China claimed 'fishing boats sheltering from weather.' Within Philippines' EEZ.

label: Sabina Shoal (Escoda Shoal) — 2024 Standoff
sub: Philippines deployed BRP Teresa Magbanua April 2024 after suspected Chinese reclamation activity. By August 2024, China deployed 40 ships including 3 PLAN warships; BRP Teresa Magbanua was rammed August 31, sustaining structural hull damage. Philippines withdrew September 15, 2024. Described as 'most violent, dangerous point' in Philippines-China ties.

label: Reed Bank (Recto Bank) — Philippine Gas Exploration
sub: Underwater atoll and the site of the most promising natural gas deposits in the Philippines' EEZ. A 2019 Chinese vessel struck and sank Philippine fishing boat FB Gem-Ver here, abandoning 22 fishermen who were rescued by Vietnamese. China has blocked Philippine gas exploration at nearby Service Contract 72 area.

label: Patag Island (Flat Island) — Philippines
sub: 0.57 ha Philippines-held cay in the Spratlys. Small garrison maintained. Located near Chinese-controlled Hughes and McKennan Reefs. Part of the contested Philippines-administered Kalayaan Island Group.

label: Lawak Island (Nanshan Island) — Philippines
sub: Philippines-held island (0.38 ha) in the Spratlys, classified as part of Kalayaan municipality in Palawan. Hosts a small military garrison. China, Taiwan, and Vietnam also claim the island. Located within Chinese missile coverage range from all three major Spratly outposts.

label: Loaita Island (Kota Island) — Philippines
sub: Philippines-administered island in the Spratlys. 0.39 ha; one of the smaller Philippine-held features. Military garrison maintained. Located adjacent to Philippines-claimed areas of the Kalayaan Island Group.

label: Likas Island (West York Island) — Philippines
sub: Philippines-held island (18.6 ha) in the northeastern Spratlys. Hosts a small airstrip and military garrison. Part of the Philippines' administrative territory under Kalayaan municipality. One of the more substantial Philippine-held Spratly features.

label: Scarborough Shoal — CCG Blockade Point
sub: China Coast Guard maintains continuous presence at the southeastern entrance to Scarborough Shoal's lagoon, blocking Filipino fishermen. Water cannon incidents: December 2023 (damaged 3 vessels), April 30, 2024 (during Balikatan exercises), October 8, 2024 (sailor injured, USNI video published). PLAN warships shadowed Philippine vessels in December 2024 for first time.

label: Puerto Princesa — Resupply Staging Base
sub: Capital of Palawan province and primary staging area for Philippine resupply missions to BRP Sierra Madre at Second Thomas Shoal. Philippine Navy and Coast Guard vessels depart from here. Distance to Second Thomas Shoal: approximately 105 nautical miles. China's blocking operations target convoys originating from this area.

label: El Nido, Palawan — Northern Patrol Base
sub: Northern Palawan town used as an additional staging area for Philippine Coast Guard and Navy patrols to the Kalayaan Island Group and Thitu Island. Also coordinates resupply missions to Pag-asa Island. Experienced growth in military presence under Marcos administration.

label: Subic Bay — Philippine Naval Base
sub: Former major US naval base (closed 1992); now Subic Bay Freeport Zone with Philippine Navy re-expanding presence. Key logistics hub for Philippine naval operations. Under Marcos administration, discussions resumed about potential return of limited US naval presence. 95 km northwest of Manila.

label: Manila — Philippine National Capital
sub: Political and military command center of the Philippines. Hosts AFP General Headquarters at Camp Aguinaldo, Philippine Coast Guard HQ, and Philippine DFA where diplomatic protests against China are filed and processed. Site of summoning Chinese Ambassador after each major SCS incident.

label: Batanes Islands — Northernmost Philippines
sub: Philippines' northernmost island group, located in the Luzon Strait separating Luzon from Taiwan (~150 km). Site of US NMESIS anti-ship missile deployment during Balikatan 2025. Strategic chokepoint for any Chinese naval transit between the Pacific and South China Sea. Coveted by US military planners for its proximity to Taiwan.

label: Second Thomas Shoal — Approach Corridor
sub: The contested maritime approach used by Philippine resupply missions to reach BRP Sierra Madre. China's CCG typically deploys 14+ vessels (as of 2023) to intercept resupply convoys along this corridor. At least 7 uses of force by China documented in the 9 months from October 2023 to June 2024 in this area.

label: Benito Ebuen Air Base — Cebu
sub: One of the original five EDCA (Enhanced Defense Cooperation Agreement) sites. Located on Mactan Island near Cebu City. Provides US forces with access to an air base strategically positioned between the Sulu Sea and South China Sea. Hosts Philippine Air Force fighter and transport units.

label: Tawi-Tawi — Southern Philippines Patrol Base
sub: Southernmost Philippine province, located near the maritime boundary with Malaysia's Sabah. Philippine Coast Guard and Navy patrol from here into the Sulu Sea. Strategically positioned to monitor Chinese maritime activity in the southern SCS and approach to the Celebes Sea.

label: Naval Base Camilo Osias — New EDCA Site
sub: One of four new EDCA sites announced February 2023 by Secretary Austin and President Marcos. Located in Santa Ana, Cagayan, northern Luzon—less than 400 km from the Bashi Channel and Taiwan. Provides US warship port access, repair facilities, and pre-positioned supplies near Taiwan and Luzon Strait.

label: Lal-Lo Airport — New EDCA Site
sub: New EDCA site in Cagayan, northern Luzon (April 2023). Alternate airfield for US fighter jet refueling and rearming. Strategically positioned to extend the range of US air operations toward Taiwan and the Luzon Strait. Three of the four new EDCA sites are in northern Luzon, facing Taiwan.

label: Camp Melchor Dela Cruz — New EDCA Site
sub: New EDCA site in Gamu, Isabela, northern Luzon (April 2023). HQ of Philippine Army's 5th Infantry Division; hosts a military hospital. Provides US land-based air defense positioning in northern Luzon. US committed to raising EDCA infrastructure investment from $82M to over $100M by end of FY2023.

label: Balabac Island — New EDCA Site
sub: New EDCA site in southernmost Palawan (April 2023). Located 330 km from the center of the Spratly Islands, dominating the Sulu Sea passage. Provides US forces with a southern base flanking the Spratly Islands. Strategically balances the northern Luzon EDCA sites to create multi-directional coverage.

label: Basa Air Base (Cesar Basa AFB)
sub: One of the original five EDCA sites since 2014. Major PAF fighter base 64 km northwest of Manila. 8,000 ft runway. First major EDCA infrastructure project (humanitarian warehouse) activated January 2019. Hosts US pre-positioned equipment and provides rapid response capability for South China Sea contingencies.

label: Antonio Bautista Air Base — Palawan EDCA
sub: One of the original five EDCA sites. Located on Palawan island, directly facing the contested West Philippine Sea. Key air base for Philippine Air Force and US rotational forces monitoring Spratly Islands and EEZ intrusions. Nearest Philippine military airfield to Second Thomas Shoal.

label: Fort Magsaysay — EDCA Ground Training Site
sub: Largest Philippine Army reservation (53,000 ha) in Nueva Ecija, central Luzon. One of the original five EDCA sites. Used for major joint ground force exercises including Balikatan. Hosts HIMARS and Patriot missile system deployments during exercises. US has invested significantly in training ranges here.

label: Andersen Air Force Base — Guam
sub: Major USAF base on Guam; key staging hub for B-1B, B-2, and B-52 bomber rotations covering the Indo-Pacific. Hosts MQ-9 drones and F-22/F-35 fighter detachments. Located ~3,000 km east of the South China Sea; within range of DF-26 'Guam Killer' missile. Continuously upgraded for a potential Taiwan contingency.

label: Naval Base Guam (Apra Harbor)
sub: Only US submarine base west of Hawaii. Hosts Los Angeles-class SSNs and Virginia-class forward-deployed submarines. Key logistics hub for US carrier and surface combatant operations in the western Pacific. Targeted by Chinese DF-26 'Guam Killer' ASBM doctrine. US investing heavily in base hardening and missile defense.

label: Cam Ranh Bay — Vietnam Naval Base
sub: Vietnam's premier deep-water naval facility; finest natural harbor in Southeast Asia. Soviet/Russian naval base 1979–2002. Vietnam People's Navy now operates from here, monitoring South China Sea disputes. US Navy port visits increasingly frequent under US-Vietnam Comprehensive Strategic Partnership (2023). Provides alternative logistics access for Western Pacific operations.

label: Singapore — US Naval Access Hub
sub: Changi Naval Base hosts US Littoral Combat Ships on rotating deployments. Singapore grants the US access under a 2005 Strategic Framework Agreement. Singapore's annual Shangri-La Dialogue is the Indo-Pacific's premier security forum. Singapore navigates carefully between US and China ties, reflecting ASEAN's broader strategic tension.

label: South China Sea — US Carrier Operating Area
sub: Approximate operating area for US carrier strike groups in the South China Sea. Dual-carrier exercises conducted July 2020 (USS Nimitz + USS Ronald Reagan) for first time since 2014. US carriers routinely operate within range of Chinese DF-21D and DF-26 anti-ship ballistic missiles. FONOPs conducted within 12 nm of Chinese-occupied Spratly Islands.

label: Luzon Strait — NMESIS Anti-Ship Deployment
sub: 3rd Marine Littoral Regiment deployed NMESIS truck-mounted Naval Strike Missile launchers to Batanes Islands in the Luzon Strait during Balikatan 2025 (April–May 2025). First overseas deployment outside the US. With ~100 nm range, NMESIS can deny Chinese naval transit through the Luzon Strait between the Pacific and South China Sea.

label: Spratly Islands — Disputed Archipelago
sub: Contested by China, Taiwan, Vietnam, Philippines, Malaysia, and Brunei. China has the largest military presence through three fully militarized artificial islands. The 2016 PCA ruling found most features generate only a 12 nm territorial sea or no maritime zone at all. Underlain by significant hydrocarbon reserves.

label: Paracel Islands — China-Controlled, Vietnam Claimed
sub: Administered by China since seizing from South Vietnam in January 1974 (Battle of the Paracel Islands; ~75 Vietnamese killed). Vietnam continues to claim as 'Hoang Sa' archipelago. China has militarized the Paracels extensively, with Woody Island hosting a runway, SAMs, fighters, and administrative center for Sansha City.

label: Natuna Islands — Indonesia-China Overlap
sub: Indonesia's northernmost islands, whose EEZ overlaps with China's nine-dash line claim. Indonesia does not consider itself an SCS claimant, but has repeatedly confronted Chinese fishing vessels and CCG ships in Natuna waters (2019, 2020, 2021). Indonesia deployed warships and F-16s after Chinese incursions. Beijing claims 'traditional fishing rights' in Indonesian EEZ.

label: Vanguard Bank — Vietnam EEZ Dispute
sub: Underwater bank within Vietnam's claimed EEZ, near significant gas deposits. China's Haiyang Dizhi 8 survey vessel operated in Vanguard Bank area from July–October 2019, escorted by CCG ships, prompting Vietnamese and Chinese vessel standoffs. US State Department (July 2020) specifically cited Vanguard Bank as an area where China has 'no lawful maritime claim.'

label: James Shoal — China's Southernmost Claim
sub: Submerged bank ~80 km off the coast of Malaysian Sarawak. China claims as its southernmost territorial limit based on the nine-dash line, despite the shoal being permanently underwater (not an island). UNCLOS does not recognize sovereignty over submerged features. China's PLAN has conducted oath-taking ceremonies here. Within Malaysia's EEZ.

label: Luconia Shoals — Malaysia EEZ
sub: Located within Malaysia's EEZ, the Luconia Shoals have been subject to persistent Chinese Coast Guard presence since 2013. Malaysia quietly protests but avoids public confrontation. US (July 2020) specifically cited Luconia Shoals as an area where China has 'no lawful maritime claim.' Home to significant gas reserves exploited by PETRONAS.

label: Brunei EEZ — Chinese Overlap Zone
sub: China's nine-dash line overlaps with Brunei's entire offshore EEZ, encompassing Brunei's productive hydrocarbon fields. Brunei is an ASEAN member and SCS claimant that has avoided direct confrontation with China. US (July 2020) cited Brunei's EEZ as an area where China's nine-dash line claim has no legal basis.

label: Nine-Dash Line — Central Contested Zone
sub: China's claimed boundary encompasses approximately 90% of the South China Sea. The PCA (2016) found it has 'no legal basis' under UNCLOS to the extent it exceeds permitted entitlements. The US formally rejected the claim July 13, 2020. The nine-dash line overlaps with the EEZs of the Philippines, Vietnam, Malaysia, Brunei, and Indonesia.

label: Lombok Strait — AUKUS Submarine Transit
sub: Strategic deep-water strait between Bali and Lombok, Indonesia. Primary transit route for nuclear-powered submarines avoiding the shallow Malacca Strait. AUKUS submarines from HMAS Stirling (Western Australia) would transit through Lombok Strait to reach the South China Sea. Indonesia does not permit foreign nuclear-powered submarines in its territorial waters, requiring deep-water transits.

label: Strait of Malacca — Strategic Chokepoint
sub: World's most strategically important shipping lane: 85,000+ transits per year, 30%+ of global trade. One-third of world's oil supply transits through here. A major conflict in the South China Sea could disrupt Malacca Strait traffic, triggering global economic crisis. China's 'Malacca Dilemma': dependence on this US Navy-secured strait makes it a strategic vulnerability.

label: Singapore — AUKUS Diplomatic & Logistics Hub
sub: Host of annual Shangri-La Dialogue (IISS Asia Security Summit). US has rotating Littoral Combat Ships at Changi Naval Base. Key neutral diplomatic space for US-China and AUKUS engagement. Singapore has expressed concern about being 'forced to choose' between US and China while maintaining military cooperation with both.

label: Luzon Strait — First Island Chain Chokepoint
sub: ~250 km wide between Luzon (Philippines) and Taiwan. Critical chokepoint for PLAN surface and submarine transit between the South China Sea and Pacific Ocean. 3rd Marine Littoral Regiment with NMESIS deployed here during Balikatan 2025 to demonstrate US ability to deny PLAN freedom of movement. Key battleground in any Taiwan contingency.

label: Balikatan 2024 Exercise Zone — Outside Territorial Waters
sub: Balikatan 2024 (April–May 2024) marked the first time exercises were held outside the Philippines' 12 nm territorial waters, directly challenging Chinese EEZ claims. France participated as full partner for the first time. 16,000 troops from US, Philippines, Australia, France. A live-fire missile test struck a target vessel. China protested the exercises.

label: Taiwan Strait — China's Declared 'Internal Waters'
sub: China claims the Taiwan Strait as 'internal waters,' rejecting the traditional median line concept. PLA conducted record incursions across the median line: 23 total crossings before Pelosi visit (Aug 2022), then 563 in 2022 alone. Multiple PLA exercise encirclements (2022, 2023, 2024) simulated Taiwan blockade scenarios. US maintains freedom of navigation transits through the strait.

label: Lumbia Air Base — Mindanao EDCA Site
sub: One of the original five EDCA sites since 2014, located in Cagayan de Oro, Mindanao. Provides US forces with access to the southern Philippines, relevant for operations in the Sulu Sea and toward the southern Spratly Islands. Philippine Air Force and US rotational forces use the base for exercises and contingency pre-positioning.

label: Tree Island (Ile des Arbres) — China Paracel Outpost
sub: Small island in the Paracel Islands controlled by China since 1974. Part of the Crescent Group of the Paracels; hosts a Chinese military garrison and monitoring equipment. Previously occupied by South Vietnam; China seized all Paracel Islands in the Battle of the Paracel Islands, January 19, 1974.

label: Phu Lam (Yongxing) — Vietnam's Claimed 'Hoang Sa'
sub: Woody Island is the administrative center of China's Sansha City but is also claimed by Vietnam as part of 'Hoang Sa' (Paracel Islands). Vietnam's official position is that China's 1974 seizure was an illegal act of aggression, and Vietnam has never relinquished its claim. Vietnam periodically protests Chinese administrative actions on this island.

label: Recto Bank — 2019 FB Gem-Ver Sinking
sub: Chinese fishing vessel F/V Yuemaobinyu 42212 struck and sank Philippine fishing boat FB Gem-Ver at Reed Bank (Recto Bank) on June 9, 2019, abandoning 22 Filipino fishermen. Vietnamese fishermen rescued the crew. The incident triggered massive political backlash against Duterte's pro-China stance and remains a flashpoint for Philippine public opinion on the South China Sea.
